topics.concept_summary:
Classes I and II are very important in school education, considered as the foundation. Learning at higher classes depends on skills of Language and Mathematics acquired at the primary stage. Children have some mathematical concepts before they come to school, and the learning of mathematics must be built on the foundation of the concepts known to them.

Children use mathematics in their daily life situations. They estimate, calculate and compare quantities in an informal way and in meaningful situations. The textbooks for classes I and II were prepared with a view to bidding farewell to rote learning and beginning to learn mathematics meaningfully.

Units have been prepared in such a manner that pupils knowledge construction through investigation, understanding of mathematical concepts through observation, confirmation and generalisation in accordance with the basic principles laid down in National Curriculum Framework 2005 and Right to Education 2009.

Every unit includes the mathematical concepts known to pupils and pre mathematical concepts for the unit concerned besides appropriate exercises. The exercises have been prepared to ensure introduction of concepts through day-to-day / meaningful situations, to get pupils to solve problems that involve logical thinking, to express ideas in mathematical language and so on.

Exercises and activities are so planned that by the end of class I, pupils will be able to understand the concepts of two digit number, acquire the ability to add and subtract numbers, by the end of class II, they will be able to add numbers with regrouping, subtract numbers using the technique of borrowing, acquire the basic concepts of multiplication and division and so on.

At the beginning of any unit, the pupils must be helped to observe the pictures given. Questions must be asked to test their previous knowledge of mathematical concepts concerned. The concepts of the unit must be introduced accordingly. In this process, locally-available objects like pebbles, seeds, sticks, beads etc., must be made use of. It must be orgonised as a whole classroom activity.

Group activities must be orgonised to solve problems in a systematic manner, to think logically, to estimate things and other exercises. The textbook includes certain instructions / suggestions for the teacher. Those instructions must be followed to take up questioning the pupils, discussing things with them, getting them to observe pictures, counting and recording information etc.

Encourage pupils to understand the instructions given for problems before they can solve the problems by themselves. The textbooks have been prepared to help the pupils to take up exploration observation, research, confirmation etc., to understand mathematical concepts and apply the knowledge for solving problems.

A number of pictures depicting pupil’s real life situations have been included. Children use mathematics extensively in many day-to-day situations. Consequently they acquire ability of application. As these textbooks have been prepared with this background, they are to be utilised completely and ensure utilistion of children’s learning time.

Pupils can observe things and speak about them. They can use pre mathematical terms in their speech. They can use terms like inside - outside, above - below, fat - thin, big - small, more - less, deep - shallow and express them in their own words, giving examples and identify differences.

The concept of 'Four' is introduced by showing a picture and asking the student to count the number of items in the picture that represent 'four'. The student then writes the number '4' in the blank boxes provided.

topics.topic_name: Counting from 10 to 20 using sticks and bundles
topics.concept_summary: This section introduces numbers from 10 to 20 using bundles of ten sticks and individual sticks. The core concept is to visually represent numbers as a combination of tens and ones. By counting bundles (tens) and individual sticks (ones), children can understand the composition of each number between 10 and 20. This hands-on approach helps solidify the understanding of place value.

The number 10 is represented as one bundle of ten sticks. This emphasizes that 10 is a group of ten individual units. This representation is crucial for understanding place value, where the position of a digit determines its value. In this case, '1' in the tens place represents one group of ten.

Numbers 11 and 12 are represented using one bundle of ten sticks and one or two individual sticks, respectively. This builds upon the concept of representing 10 and extends it to numbers slightly greater than 10. It reinforces the idea that these numbers are composed of one ten and some ones.

Numbers 13 to 16 are represented using one bundle of ten sticks and three to six individual sticks, respectively. This continues to reinforce the concept of place value and the composition of numbers between 10 and 20. Children learn to associate the visual representation with the numerical value and the corresponding word.

Numbers 17 to 19 are represented using one bundle of ten sticks and seven to nine individual sticks, respectively. This further solidifies the understanding of place value and the composition of numbers between 10 and 20. The consistent use of bundles and individual sticks helps children visualize the numerical values.

The number 20 is represented as two bundles of ten sticks. This introduces the concept of having more than one group of ten. It reinforces the understanding of place value, where '2' in the tens place represents two groups of ten.

topics.topic_name: Counting, Showing, and Writing Numbers up to 9
topics.concept_summary: Pupils can count things up to 9 and show them with number and also write them in order.
Pupils can identify things of equal number.
Pupils can arrange the things in a sequence according to their number.
Pupils can say the first, last, middle, previous, next things which are in order within 9 as per their place.

topics.topic_name: Comparing Things as More, Less, and Equal
topics.concept_summary: Pupils can compare things (up to 9) in two groups as more, less and equal.

topics.topic_name: Arranging Numbers in Ascending and Descending Order
topics.concept_summary: Pupils can arrange numbers and things up to 9 in ascending and descending orders.
Pupils can show zero when there are not any thing. They can say zero before 1.

chapters.chapter_title: Addition of Numbers, the Total not Exceeding 9

topics.concept_summary: Pupils can say that if 1 is added to a number, we get the next number.
Pupils can count each bead in a chain and add them.
Pupils can write two numbers (up to 9) horizontally or vertically and add them.
Pupils can say how much is to be added to a number to get desired number.
Pupils can add things in two different groups and their total.

topics.topic_name: Adding Different Numbers to Get the Same Total
topics.concept_summary: They can add different numbers to get the same total.
They understand that zero added to any number gives the same number.
Pupils can say how many things must be taken from 9 and also say how many remain.
They can say that if 0 things are subtracted from a number up to 9 there will be no change and that the same number of things remain.
They can compare groups and say less or more and by how much through subtraction.
They can say zero things are there when all subtracted from given things.
They can write numbers horizontally or vertically and subtract them.
They can identify different subtractions with same result and also say them.
Pupils can say tens and ones for those more than 10.

topics.topic_name: Counting and Writing Numbers up to 20
topics.concept_summary: Pupils can count and write number up to 20 in order.
They can say which is bigger and which is smaller among any numbers up to 20 are given.
They can say the smallest and the biggest number among the given numbers.

topics.topic_name: Addition Using Bundles and Loose Sticks
topics.concept_summary: Using bundle, loose sticks pupil able to do addition of two numbers sum not exeeding 20.
Pupil able to do row and column addition of numbers.

topics.topic_name: Subtracting Single Digit from Two Digit Numbers
topics.concept_summary: Pupil able to subtract single digit number from two digit number by using bundles and loose sticks.
Pupil able to do column and row subtractions.
Pupil count stick bundles in tens from 10 to 100 and able understand and write them (10, 20, 30, ....... etc).

topics.topic_name: Showing Numbers as Tens and Ones
topics.concept_summary: Pupils can show given numbers as tens and ones using currency notes or bundles of sticks and loose sticks.

topics.topic_name: Tens and Ones in Bundles and Currency
topics.concept_summary: Pupils can say how many tens and how many ones are their in bundle of sticks and loose sticks or ten rupee and one rupee coins.
They can say/ write smaller, bigger or the middle numbers among those below 100.
They can write numbers from smaller to bigger and bigger to smaller in order.

chapters.chapter_title: Number that come Before, Between and After

topics.concept_summary: Identifying the numbers that come before, between, and after a given number.
Pupils can identify different coins (50 p, Re.1, Rs.2, Rs. 5)
They can say two 50-paise coins make one rupee
They can identify notes of denominations 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50 and 100.

This section introduces different coins and currency notes. It focuses on familiarizing students with the visual appearance and value of each coin (50 paise, 1 rupee, 2 rupees, 5 rupees) and note (5 rupees, 10 rupees, 20 rupees, 50 rupees). The goal is for students to be able to identify and differentiate between these denominations.

This section focuses on identifying the one-rupee coin. Students are asked to tick the box under the one-rupee coin to reinforce their recognition of its appearance and value. This exercise helps solidify their understanding of basic currency denominations.
original_exercises: Tick ª9μ in the box under the one-rupee coin.

topics.concept_summary: This section focuses on identifying the five-rupee coin. Students are asked to tick the box under the five-rupee coin to reinforce their recognition of its appearance and value. This exercise helps solidify their understanding of basic currency denominations.
original_exercises: Tick  ª9μ in the box under the five-rupee coin.

topics.concept_summary: This section focuses on identifying the five-rupee note. Students are asked to tick the box under the five-rupee note to reinforce their recognition of its appearance and value. This exercise helps solidify their understanding of basic currency denominations.
original_exercises: Tick ª9μ in the box under the five-rupee note.

topics.concept_summary: This section focuses on identifying the ten-rupee note. Students are asked to tick the box under the ten-rupee note to reinforce their recognition of its appearance and value. This exercise helps solidify their understanding of basic currency denominations.
original_exercises: Tick  ª9μ in the box under the ten-rupee note.

topics.concept_summary: This section involves observing images related to money and verbally expressing understanding. The specific content of the images and the expected verbal responses are not detailed, but the focus is on developing observational and communication skills related to monetary concepts.
original_exercises: Observe and say.

Students are expected to observe the value of the notes and the total value of the coins, then match the amounts that are equal. This exercise reinforces the concept of equivalent values and basic addition skills.

topics.concept_summary: Pupils can identify different times of a complete day (morning, afternoon, daytime, evening, night). They can say what people at those times.
They learn what they must do at what time

This section introduces the concept of time and its relation to daily activities. Students are presented with pictures and asked to identify what people are doing and at what time. The goal is to help them understand the timings of morning, evening, and night and associate specific activities with those times.

This section focuses on understanding the sequence of events in a day. Students are asked questions about what comes after morning and afternoon, what people do at different times, and to describe their own daily routines from morning to night. The aim is to reinforce the concepts of early morning, morning, afternoon, evening, and night.

topics.topic_name: Matching Activities with Time of Day
topics.concept_summary: This section involves matching activities with the appropriate time of day. Students are presented with pictures of activities and asked to match them with the corresponding time period (e.g., morning, afternoon, evening). This exercise reinforces the association between specific activities and the time of day they typically occur.
original_exercises:
Match the following.

Example

topics.concept_summary: This section focuses on ordering events according to the sequence in which they occur. Students are presented with pictures depicting a series of actions and asked to number them in the correct order (1, 2, 3). This exercise helps develop their understanding of cause and effect and the chronological order of events.
original_exercises:
Look at the pictures given below.  Order them according to the
action that takes place from first to the last (as 1, 2 and 3).

topics.concept_summary: Pupils can measure things using nonstandard units like foot, cubit etc.
They can say which is heavier or which is lighter, which contains more liquid
They can estimate length, weight and size without actual measurement
They can say which is more / size

This section introduces non-standard units of measurement, such as hand-breadth, handspan, cubit, and foot. Students are shown pictures of people measuring objects using these body parts. The goal is to familiarize them with the concept of measurement using readily available tools and to understand that measurements can vary depending on the size of the measuring tool.

This section focuses on comparing the weight of different objects. Students are presented with pairs of objects and asked to identify the heavier one. This exercise helps develop their understanding of relative weight and the ability to make comparisons based on visual observation.
original_exercises: Tick ª9μ the heavier one.

topics.concept_summary: This section focuses on comparing the volume of different containers. Students are presented with multiple vessels and asked to identify the one that holds more or less liquid. This exercise helps develop their understanding of relative volume and the ability to make comparisons based on visual observation.
original_exercises: Tick ª9μ the vessel which holds more water among the three.
Tick  ª9μ the vessel which holds less coffee among the three.

topics.topic_name: Measuring Distance with Cubits and Sticks
topics.concept_summary: This section involves measuring distance using both cubits and sticks. Students are asked to leap forward and then measure the distance using both units. They then record their observations in a table. This activity helps them understand the concept of measurement and compare the results obtained using different units.
original_exercises:

topics.concept_summary: This section focuses on estimating the length of objects using a pencil as a tool. Students are asked to guess how long a slate and a table are in terms of pencils and then check their guesses by actual measurement. This activity helps develop their estimation skills and reinforces the concept of measurement.
original_exercises:
Guess how long are a slate and a table, in terms of a pencil as a tool.
Check your guessing by actual measurement using a pencil.

topics.concept_summary: This section focuses on comparing the length of various objects to a pencil. Students are asked to identify which objects are shorter than a pencil. This exercise reinforces their understanding of relative length and the ability to make comparisons using a standard reference object.
original_exercises:
Look at the things given below.  Tick ª9μ the things which are shorter
than a pencil.

topics.concept_summary: This section focuses on guessing the weight of vegetables. Students are presented with pairs of vegetables and asked to guess which is lighter and which is heavier. This exercise helps develop their understanding of relative weight and the ability to make comparisons based on visual observation and prior experience.
original_exercises:
Look at the two pairs of vegetables.  Guess which of the two is light
and which is heavy.

topics.concept_summary: This section focuses on comparing the speed at which different vessels can fill a bucket. Students are asked to guess which vessel would fill the bucket faster and then verify their guess by actual filling. This activity helps develop their understanding of volume and the relationship between vessel size and filling speed.
original_exercises:
If you want to fill the bucket fast, which vessel should you use?
Guess.  Find out by actual filling.
